---
title: "BERT vs GPT-4 for Sentiment Analysis"
description: "Fine-tuning BERT on Rotten Tomatoes reviews and comparing performance against GPT-4 zero-shot and few-shot prompting for business NLP applications."
author: "Raunit Budhiraja"
date: "2025-04-10"
image: "../../images/project-bert.png"
categories: [Python, NLP, BERT, Machine Learning, Deep Learning]
page-layout: full
toc: true
code-fold: true
code-tools: true
highlight-style: dracula
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: true
---

## Overview

Two fundamentally different types of AI model are compared here. BERT is a **discriminative** model — built to classify. GPT-4 is a **generative** model — built to produce language, which can be directed toward classification through prompting.[^1]

[^1]: This distinction — discriminative vs generative — is one of the most important conceptual divides in modern NLP. Discriminative models learn a boundary between classes; generative models learn the underlying data distribution.

Understanding this distinction is essential before drawing any conclusions about which approach is better for a given business context.

---

## Why This Comparison Matters

For a company processing thousands of customer reviews per day, the choice of model has real operational consequences:

| Consideration | BERT (fine-tuned) | GPT-4 (prompting) |
|---|---|---|
| Setup cost | High — requires labelled data & training | Low — just write a prompt |
| Inference cost | Low — runs locally | High — API cost per call |
| Latency | Fast (~10ms/sample) | Slow (~1–3s/sample) |
| Accuracy | High on in-domain data | Strong zero-shot, improves with examples |
| Flexibility | Retrain for new tasks | Prompt engineering only |

---

## Model Pipeline

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Rotten Tomatoes Dataset] --> B[Text Cleaning]
  B --> C[Balanced 20k Sample]
  C --> D1[BERT Tokeniser]
  C --> D2[GPT-4 API Prompt]
  D1 --> E1[Fine-tune BERT]
  D2 --> E2[Zero-Shot]
  D2 --> E3[Few-Shot]
  E1 --> F[Compare on 200-sample Test Set]
  E2 --> F
  E3 --> F
  F --> G[Metrics: Accuracy, F1, Latency]
```

---

## Evaluation Metric

The primary metric is **F1-score**, which balances precision and recall — important when both false positives and false negatives carry a cost:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

ROC-AUC is used as a secondary metric:

$$\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(t) \, d\text{FPR}(t)$$

---

## Setup

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import torch
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import (
    accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score,
    f1_score, classification_report, confusion_matrix
)
from transformers import (
    BertTokenizer, BertForSequenceClassification,
    TrainingArguments, Trainer, EarlyStoppingCallback
)
from torch.utils.data import Dataset

device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
print(f'Using device: {device}')
```

---

## Data Loading and Preparation

```{python}
# Load Rotten Tomatoes critic reviews dataset
df_raw = pd.read_csv('data/rotten_tomatoes_critic_reviews.csv')

TEXT_COL  = 'review_content'
LABEL_COL = 'review_type'
LABEL_MAP = {'Fresh': 1, 'Rotten': 0}

df = df_raw[[TEXT_COL, LABEL_COL]].dropna().copy()
df.columns = ['text', 'label_raw']
df['label'] = df['label_raw'].map(LABEL_MAP)
df = df.dropna(subset=['label'])
df['label'] = df['label'].astype(int)

# Balanced 10k per class
SAMPLE_PER_CLASS = 10_000
df_pos = df[df['label'] == 1].sample(n=SAMPLE_PER_CLASS, random_state=42)
df_neg = df[df['label'] == 0].sample(n=SAMPLE_PER_CLASS, random_state=42)
df_balanced = pd.concat([df_pos, df_neg]).sample(frac=1, random_state=42).reset_index(drop=True)

print(f'Dataset size: {len(df_balanced):,}')
print(df_balanced['label'].value_counts())
```

---

## Text Cleaning

```{python}
import re

def clean_text(text: str) -> str:
    text = str(text).lower()
    text = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', ' ', text)
    text = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9\s\'\"\.,!?-]', ' ', text)
    text = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', text).strip()
    return text

df_balanced['text_clean'] = df_balanced['text'].apply(clean_text)
print('Sample cleaned review:')
print(df_balanced['text_clean'].iloc[0][:300])
```

---

## Train/Val/Test Split

```{python}
texts  = df_balanced['text_clean'].tolist()
labels = df_balanced['label'].tolist()

# 70% train / 15% val / 15% test
train_val_texts, test_texts, train_val_labels, test_labels = train_test_split(
    texts, labels, test_size=0.15, random_state=42, stratify=labels
)
train_texts, val_texts, train_labels, val_labels = train_test_split(
    train_val_texts, train_val_labels,
    test_size=0.176, random_state=42, stratify=train_val_labels
)

print(f'Train: {len(train_texts):,} | Val: {len(val_texts):,} | Test: {len(test_texts):,}')
```

---

## BERT Tokenisation and Dataset

```{python}
MODEL_NAME = 'bert-base-uncased'
MAX_LEN    = 128

tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME)

class SentimentDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, encodings, labels):
        self.encodings = encodings
        self.labels    = labels
    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.labels)
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        item = {key: val[idx] for key, val in self.encodings.items()}
        item['labels'] = torch.tensor(self.labels[idx], dtype=torch.long)
        return item

train_encodings = tokenizer(train_texts, truncation=True,
    padding='max_length', max_length=MAX_LEN, return_tensors='pt')
val_encodings   = tokenizer(val_texts,   truncation=True,
    padding='max_length', max_length=MAX_LEN, return_tensors='pt')
test_encodings  = tokenizer(test_texts,  truncation=True,
    padding='max_length', max_length=MAX_LEN, return_tensors='pt')

train_dataset = SentimentDataset(train_encodings, train_labels)
val_dataset   = SentimentDataset(val_encodings,   val_labels)
test_dataset  = SentimentDataset(test_encodings,  test_labels)
```

---

## Fine-Tuning BERT

```{python}
model = BertForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    MODEL_NAME, num_labels=2,
    id2label={0: 'NEGATIVE', 1: 'POSITIVE'},
    label2id={'NEGATIVE': 0, 'POSITIVE': 1}
).to(device)

def compute_metrics(eval_pred):
    logits, labels = eval_pred
    preds = np.argmax(logits, axis=-1)
    return {
        'accuracy' : round(accuracy_score(labels, preds), 4),
        'f1'       : round(f1_score(labels, preds, average='binary'), 4),
        'precision': round(precision_score(labels, preds, average='binary'), 4),
        'recall'   : round(recall_score(labels, preds, average='binary'), 4),
    }

training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir='./bert_results',
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    per_device_train_batch_size=16,
    per_device_eval_batch_size=32,
    num_train_epochs=3,
    weight_decay=0.01,
    warmup_steps=500,
    eval_strategy='epoch',
    save_strategy='epoch',
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    metric_for_best_model='f1',
    seed=42,
    fp16=torch.cuda.is_available(),
    report_to='none',
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model, args=training_args,
    train_dataset=train_dataset, eval_dataset=val_dataset,
    compute_metrics=compute_metrics,
    callbacks=[EarlyStoppingCallback(early_stopping_patience=2)]
)

train_result = trainer.train()
print(f"Final train loss: {train_result.metrics['train_loss']:.4f}")
```

---

## GPT-4 Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Prompting

```{python}
import openai
import time

client = openai.OpenAI(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

# Zero-shot prompt
ZERO_SHOT_TEMPLATE = """Classify the sentiment of this movie review as exactly one word: POSITIVE or NEGATIVE.

Review: {review}

Answer:"""

# Few-shot examples
FEW_SHOT_EXAMPLES = [
    {"review": "A visually stunning masterpiece.", "label": "POSITIVE"},
    {"review": "Heartfelt performances and a tightly written script.", "label": "POSITIVE"},
    {"review": "A feel-good crowd-pleaser.", "label": "POSITIVE"},
    {"review": "A tedious, poorly paced slog.", "label": "NEGATIVE"},
    {"review": "Confusing, loud, and devoid of emotional resonance.", "label": "NEGATIVE"},
    {"review": "Collapses under its own pretension.", "label": "NEGATIVE"},
]

def build_few_shot_prompt(review: str) -> str:
    examples = "\n".join(
        f'Review: {ex["review"]}\nSentiment: {ex["label"]}'
        for ex in FEW_SHOT_EXAMPLES
    )
    return (f"Classify each review as POSITIVE or NEGATIVE.\n\n"
            f"{examples}\n\nReview: {review[:400]}\nSentiment:")

def gpt4_predict(prompt: str) -> int:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        max_tokens=5, temperature=0
    )
    reply = response.choices[0].message.content.strip().upper()
    return 1 if "POSITIVE" in reply else 0
```

---

## Three-Way Comparison Results

```{python}
# Sample 200 from test set for comparison
N = 200
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
idx = rng.choice(len(test_texts), N, replace=False)
sample_texts  = [test_texts[i]  for i in idx]
sample_labels = [test_labels[i] for i in idx]

# BERT predictions
sample_enc = tokenizer(sample_texts, truncation=True,
    padding='max_length', max_length=MAX_LEN, return_tensors='pt')
sample_ds   = SentimentDataset(sample_enc, sample_labels)
bert_output = trainer.predict(sample_ds)
bert_preds  = np.argmax(bert_output.predictions, axis=-1).tolist()

# GPT-4 zero-shot
zs_preds = [gpt4_predict(ZERO_SHOT_TEMPLATE.format(review=t[:400])) for t in sample_texts]

# GPT-4 few-shot
fs_preds = [gpt4_predict(build_few_shot_prompt(t)) for t in sample_texts]

# Results table
results = {
    'Model':     ['BERT (fine-tuned)', 'GPT-4 Zero-Shot', 'GPT-4 Few-Shot'],
    'Accuracy':  [accuracy_score(sample_labels, p) for p in [bert_preds, zs_preds, fs_preds]],
    'F1':        [f1_score(sample_labels, p, average='binary') for p in [bert_preds, zs_preds, fs_preds]],
    'Precision': [precision_score(sample_labels, p, average='binary') for p in [bert_preds, zs_preds, fs_preds]],
    'Recall':    [recall_score(sample_labels, p, average='binary') for p in [bert_preds, zs_preds, fs_preds]],
}
results_df = pd.DataFrame(results)
print(results_df.to_string(index=False))
```

---

## Visualisation

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Three-way performance comparison across Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score."

metrics = ['Accuracy', 'Precision', 'Recall', 'F1']
models  = results_df['Model'].tolist()
x = np.arange(len(metrics))
width = 0.25

colors = ['#7fff6e', '#68d9ff', '#a78bfa']
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
for i, (model, color) in enumerate(zip(models, colors)):
    vals = [results_df.loc[results_df['Model']==model, m].values[0] for m in metrics]
    bars = ax.bar(x + (i - 1) * width, vals, width, label=model,
                  color=color, edgecolor='black', alpha=0.9)
    for bar in bars:
        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.005,
                f'{bar.get_height():.3f}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8)

ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(metrics)
ax.set_ylim(0.70, 1.01)
ax.set_ylabel('Score')
ax.set_title('BERT vs GPT-4: Zero-Shot vs Few-Shot', fontweight='bold')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

---

## Project Checklist

- [x] Load and balance Rotten Tomatoes dataset
- [x] Clean and tokenise text
- [x] Fine-tune BERT with early stopping
- [x] GPT-4 zero-shot evaluation
- [x] GPT-4 few-shot evaluation (6 examples)
- [x] Three-way performance comparison
- [ ] Deploy BERT as a REST API (future work)

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.callout-note}
## What the comparison showed

- **BERT** achieves higher F1 with low latency — the right tool for high-throughput production classification.
- **GPT-4 few-shot** closes the gap significantly with just 6 labelled examples — no training required.
- **GPT-4 zero-shot** performs surprisingly well, making it useful for rapid prototyping when labelled data doesn't exist yet.
- The practical decision depends on volume, latency constraints, and budget — not just accuracy numbers.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Key insight on few-shot prompting
Few-shot examples reduce label ambiguity — the model sees exactly what format and vocabulary are expected. It's a zero-cost adaptation strategy: no GPU, no dataset labelling required.
:::

---

## Reflection

The most interesting tension in this project was between accuracy and practicality. BERT wins on pure metrics, but a business spinning up a sentiment analysis pipeline for the first time probably shouldn't start with fine-tuning. GPT-4 with a well-written few-shot prompt gets you 85–90% of the way there in an afternoon.

The harder question — which I kept coming back to — is what "accuracy" even means in a production context. A model that's 92% accurate on a balanced test set might perform very differently on the skewed, messy text that actually comes through a review system. Benchmark numbers are a starting point, not a verdict.

<br>
[← Back to Projects](../index.qmd){.btn-ghost}
